tasks.prompt:
[Warm-up] Observing Liveness Probe Behavior

Deploy a Pod running nginx:1.25 with an HTTP Liveness Probe attached, then verify that the Probe is actually firing by checking the event log.

Requirements:
- Path: /healthz (nginx does not serve this path by default, so the Probe will intentionally fail)
- initialDelaySeconds: 5, periodSeconds: 5, failureThreshold: 2
- After creating the Pod, run kubectl get pod -w and observe the RESTARTS count incrementing.
- Run kubectl describe pod and check the Events section for the Probe failure → container restart sequence.
tasks.starter:
# nginx-liveness-fail.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: nginx-liveness-fail
spec:
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx:1.25
    ports:
    - containerPort: 80
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: ___________   # fill in a path that does not exist
        port: 80
      initialDelaySeconds: ___  # 5
      periodSeconds: ___        # 5
      failureThreshold: ___     # 2
tasks.solution:
# Completed nginx-liveness-fail.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: nginx-liveness-fail
spec:
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx:1.25
    ports:
    - containerPort: 80
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /healthz
        port: 80
      initialDelaySeconds: 5
      periodSeconds: 5
      failureThreshold: 2

# Deploy and observe
kubectl apply -f nginx-liveness-fail.yaml
kubectl get pod nginx-liveness-fail -w          # watch RESTARTS count rise
kubectl describe pod nginx-liveness-fail        # check Events section

# The following sequence should appear in the Events output:
# Liveness probe failed: HTTP probe failed with statuscode: 404
# Killing  container with id ...: liveness probe failed
# Started  Container nginx

# Clean up after the exercise
kubectl delete pod nginx-liveness-fail
tasks.explanation: nginx does not serve /healthz by default, so it returns 404. Because 404 falls outside the 200–399 success range, the Probe marks the check as a failure. With failureThreshold: 2, Kubernetes forcibly terminates (Killing) the container after two consecutive failures and starts a new one (Started). This restart increments the RESTARTS counter. The important detail is that the Pod itself remains alive — only the container restarts — so the Pod's IP address does not change. This exercise lets you trace the full path from a Liveness Probe failure to a container restart directly in the Events output.
hints: Any path that nginx does not serve works here. The default nginx image has no /healthz route, so it returns 404.
Leave kubectl get pod nginx-liveness-fail -w running; the STATUS and RESTARTS columns update in real time. After roughly 15–20 seconds, RESTARTS should reach 1 or higher.
Scroll to the bottom of kubectl describe pod nginx-liveness-fail and look at the Events: section. You should see 'Liveness probe failed', 'Killing', and 'Started' appearing in that order.

tasks.prompt:
[Core Lab 1] Configuring a Readiness Probe and Observing Traffic Blocking

Deploy an nginx Deployment (replicas: 2) with a Readiness Probe, then intentionally trigger a Readiness failure on one Pod and verify that it is removed from the Service endpoints.

Steps:
1. Complete the starter below and deploy both the Deployment and a ClusterIP Service.
2. Run kubectl get endpoints <service-name> and confirm two endpoints are listed.
3. Exec into one of the running Pods and delete /tmp/ready (the exec Readiness Probe checks for this file).
4. Run kubectl get pod -w and watch the targeted Pod's READY column change from 1/1 to 0/1.
5. Run kubectl get endpoints again and confirm the endpoint count has dropped to 1.
6. Recreate /tmp/ready inside the Pod and verify the Pod returns to Ready.
tasks.starter:
# readiness-demo.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: readiness-demo
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: readiness-demo
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: readiness-demo
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: busybox:1.36
        command: ["/bin/sh", "-c",
          "touch /tmp/ready && while true; do sleep 30; done"]
        readinessProbe:
          exec:
            command:
            - cat
            - ___________   # fill in the file path to check (/tmp/ready)
          initialDelaySeconds: 3
          periodSeconds: 5
          failureThreshold: 2
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: readiness-svc
spec:
  selector:
    app: readiness-demo
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 80
tasks.solution:
# 1. Complete the starter (fill in the path)
# command: ["cat", "/tmp/ready"]

# Completed readiness-demo.yaml (key section)
        readinessProbe:
          exec:
            command:
            - cat
            - /tmp/ready
          initialDelaySeconds: 3
          periodSeconds: 5
          failureThreshold: 2

# 2. Deploy
kubectl apply -f readiness-demo.yaml

# 3. Confirm initial endpoints (should show 2 IPs)
kubectl get endpoints readiness-svc

# 4. Get Pod name, exec in, and delete the file
kubectl get pod -l app=readiness-demo
# Example: POD_NAME=readiness-demo-xxxxxxx-yyyyy
kubectl exec -it <POD_NAME> -- rm /tmp/ready

# 5. Watch Pod status (separate terminal recommended)
kubectl get pod -l app=readiness-demo -w
# → READY column should change from 1/1 to 0/1

# 6. Re-check endpoints (should drop to 1)
kubectl get endpoints readiness-svc

# 7. Restore the file → Pod returns to Ready
kubectl exec -it <POD_NAME> -- touch /tmp/ready
kubectl get endpoints readiness-svc
# → Should return to 2 endpoints

# Clean up
kubectl delete -f readiness-demo.yaml
tasks.explanation: A Readiness Probe failure does not restart the container. Instead, Kubernetes removes the Pod from the Service's endpoint list so that no traffic is directed to it. Deleting /tmp/ready causes 'cat /tmp/ready' to return exit code 1 (file not found), which the Probe treats as a failure. With failureThreshold: 2, the Pod transitions to Not Ready after about 10 seconds (periodSeconds 5 × 2 failures) and is dropped from the endpoints. Recreating the file causes the Probe to succeed and the Pod is automatically added back to the endpoints. This is the core Readiness behavior: blocking and restoring traffic without any container restart.
hints: In an exec Probe, the last element of the command array is the file path to check. Running 'cat /tmp/ready' returns exit code 0 (success) if the file exists and exit code 1 (failure) if it does not.
Storing the Pod name in a variable saves typing: POD_NAME=$(kubectl get pod -l app=readiness-demo -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')

kubectl get pod -w streams status changes. Wait about 10–15 seconds after running rm; the READY column should drop to 0/1. Immediately after, run kubectl get endpoints readiness-svc to confirm the IP was removed.

tasks.prompt:
[Core Lab 2] Combined Liveness + Readiness Probes with a change-cause Annotation

Create an apps/v1 Deployment with both a Liveness Probe (httpGet) and a Readiness Probe (httpGet) configured, and record a change-cause annotation on the initial deployment.

Requirements:
- Image: nginx:1.25, replicas: 3
- Liveness: GET /index.html :80, initialDelaySeconds: 10, periodSeconds: 10, failureThreshold: 3
- Readiness: GET /index.html :80, initialDelaySeconds: 5, periodSeconds: 5, failureThreshold: 2
- Add the annotation kubernetes.io/change-cause: "v1 initial deployment - nginx 1.25" to metadata.annotations
- After deploying, check the rollout status with kubectl rollout status and inspect ReplicaSets with kubectl get rs.
tasks.starter:
# probe-deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: probe-demo
  annotations:
    kubernetes.io/change-cause: ___________   # fill in the change description
spec:
  replicas: 3
  revisionHistoryLimit: 5
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: probe-demo
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: probe-demo
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:1.25
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /index.html
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: ___   # 10
          periodSeconds: ___         # 10
          failureThreshold: ___      # 3
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /index.html
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: ___   # 5
          periodSeconds: ___         # 5
          failureThreshold: ___      # 2
tasks.solution:
# Completed probe-deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: probe-demo
  annotations:
    kubernetes.io/change-cause: "v1 initial deployment - nginx 1.25"
spec:
  replicas: 3
  revisionHistoryLimit: 5
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: probe-demo
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: probe-demo
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:1.25
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /index.html
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 10
          periodSeconds: 10
          failureThreshold: 3
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /index.html
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 5
          failureThreshold: 2

# Deploy
kubectl apply -f probe-deployment.yaml

# Confirm rollout completion (look for 'successfully rolled out')
kubectl rollout status deployment/probe-demo

# Inspect ReplicaSets (should show 1 RS with DESIRED=READY=3)
kubectl get rs -l app=probe-demo

# View deployment history (CHANGE-CAUSE column should show the annotation)
kubectl rollout history deployment/probe-demo
tasks.explanation: Running Liveness and Readiness together is the production best practice. Readiness must succeed before traffic flows to a new Pod; after that, if the container becomes unresponsive during operation, Liveness triggers an automatic restart. Setting initialDelaySeconds lower for Readiness (5s) than for Liveness (10s) prevents Liveness from checking too early and misidentifying a healthy startup as a failure. The --record flag is deprecated, so the kubernetes.io/change-cause annotation should be placed directly in metadata.annotations. revisionHistoryLimit: 5 caps the number of stored ReplicaSets at 5 so they do not accumulate indefinitely.
hints: The change-cause annotation must go under the Deployment's metadata.annotations, not under template.metadata.annotations — otherwise it will not appear in rollout history. Do not mix up the two locations.
kubectl rollout status blocks until the deployment is complete. When you see '3 out of 3 new replicas have been updated' and 'successfully rolled out', the rollout is done.
kubectl get rs -l app=probe-demo should show exactly one ReplicaSet with DESIRED/CURRENT/READY all equal to 3. Since this is the first deployment, there should be no old RS with 0 replicas.

tasks.prompt:
[Core Lab 3] Adjusting maxSurge / maxUnavailable and Observing a Rolling Update

Update the image in the previous exercise (probe-demo) from nginx:1.25 to nginx:1.27, setting maxSurge: 1 / maxUnavailable: 0 to perform an availability-first rolling update.

Steps:
1. Update probe-deployment.yaml: change image to nginx:1.27 and set rollingUpdate to maxSurge: 1 / maxUnavailable: 0.
2. Also update the change-cause annotation to "v2 deployment - nginx 1.27 / maxUnavailable=0 stable rollout".
3. Immediately after kubectl apply, open a second terminal and run kubectl get pod -l app=probe-demo -w. Observe the total Pod count temporarily reach 4 (3+1) before settling back to 3.
4. Run kubectl get rs -l app=probe-demo to see how DESIRED changes on both the new and old ReplicaSets.
5. Run kubectl rollout history deployment/probe-demo and confirm that two revisions (1 and 2) are listed.
tasks.starter:
# probe-deployment.yaml (updated)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: probe-demo
  annotations:
    kubernetes.io/change-cause: ___________   # fill in the v2 deployment message
spec:
  replicas: 3
  revisionHistoryLimit: 5
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: ___          # 1
      maxUnavailable: ___    # 0
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: probe-demo
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: probe-demo
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: ___________   # nginx:1.27
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /index.html
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 10
          periodSeconds: 10
          failureThreshold: 3
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /index.html
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 5
          failureThreshold: 2
tasks.solution:
# Completed probe-deployment.yaml (v2)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: probe-demo
  annotations:
    kubernetes.io/change-cause: "v2 deployment - nginx 1.27 / maxUnavailable=0 stable rollout"
spec:
  replicas: 3
  revisionHistoryLimit: 5
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1
      maxUnavailable: 0
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: probe-demo
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: probe-demo
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:1.27
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /index.html
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 10
          periodSeconds: 10
          failureThreshold: 3
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /index.html
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 5
          failureThreshold: 2

# Apply (terminal A)
kubectl apply -f probe-deployment.yaml

# Watch Pods in real time (terminal B — run immediately after apply)
kubectl get pod -l app=probe-demo -w
# Expected: total Pod count briefly rises to 4 (3 + maxSurge 1),
#           then drops back to 3 as old Pods Terminate one by one

# Check ReplicaSet state (terminal A)
kubectl get rs -l app=probe-demo
# Expected: new RS (nginx:1.27) DESIRED=3, old RS (nginx:1.25) DESIRED=0

# Wait for rollout to complete
kubectl rollout status deployment/probe-demo

# View history (should show 2 CHANGE-CAUSE entries)
kubectl rollout history deployment/probe-demo
tasks.explanation: maxUnavailable: 0 prevents Kubernetes from terminating any existing Pod before a replacement is ready. As a result, Kubernetes first creates a new Pod (nginx:1.27) up to the maxSurge: 1 limit. Only after that Pod passes the Readiness Probe and reaches Ready status does Kubernetes terminate one old Pod. Throughout the rollout, at least 3 Pods remain serviceable. By contrast, with maxUnavailable: 1 Kubernetes would terminate an old Pod first and then create a new one, causing a brief moment where only 2 Pods are available. Both approaches are valid; maxUnavailable: 0 is recommended for high-availability services. The old ReplicaSet remaining at DESIRED=0 is intentional — it is retained for rollback purposes.
hints: Open two terminals in advance. Apply from one, and start kubectl get pod -w in the other simultaneously to catch the 3→4→3 Pod count transition in time.
Setting both maxSurge and maxUnavailable to 0 makes a rollout impossible — there is no room to make any progress. At least one of them must be a positive value.

kubectl get rs -l app=probe-demo does not display the image directly in the columns. To identify the new RS, run kubectl describe rs <RS-name> | grep Image, or simply treat the RS with DESIRED=3 as the new one.

tasks.prompt:
[Applied] Intentional Deployment Failure → Rollback with rollout history + rollout undo

Update probe-demo (currently nginx:1.27) with a bad image (nginx:broken-image) to produce a stalled rolling update. Then inspect the revision history and roll back to a specific revision (revision 2, i.e., nginx:1.27).

Steps:
1. Change image to nginx:broken-image and change-cause to "v3 deployment - bad image (intentional failure for lab)" and kubectl apply.
2. Confirm that kubectl rollout status is hanging. (Press Ctrl+C to exit the watch.)
3. Run kubectl get pod -l app=probe-demo and look for Pods in ImagePullBackOff / ErrImagePull state.
4. Run kubectl rollout history deployment/probe-demo and confirm three revisions are listed.
5. Run kubectl rollout undo deployment/probe-demo --to-revision=2 to roll back to revision 2 (nginx:1.27).
6. Verify the rollback is complete with kubectl rollout status and kubectl get pod.
tasks.starter:
# Step 1: Deploy with the bad image (edit probe-deployment.yaml as follows)
#   annotations.kubernetes.io/change-cause: "v3 deployment - bad image (intentional failure for lab)"
#   image: nginx:broken-image
# Keep all other Probe/strategy fields the same as before

# Step 2: Observation commands (fill in the blanks)
kubectl apply -f probe-deployment.yaml
kubectl rollout status deployment/___________
kubectl get pod -l app=probe-demo

# Step 3: View history
kubectl rollout history deployment/___________

# Step 4: Roll back to a specific revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/___________ --to-revision=___
tasks.solution:
# Completed probe-deployment.yaml (v3 - intentional failure)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: probe-demo
  annotations:
    kubernetes.io/change-cause: "v3 deployment - bad image (intentional failure for lab)"
spec:
  replicas: 3
  revisionHistoryLimit: 5
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1
      maxUnavailable: 0
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: probe-demo
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: probe-demo
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:broken-image
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /index.html
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 10
          periodSeconds: 10
          failureThreshold: 3
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /index.html
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 5
          failureThreshold: 2

# 1. Deploy the bad image
kubectl apply -f probe-deployment.yaml

# 2. Check rollout status (will hang → press Ctrl+C to exit)
kubectl rollout status deployment/probe-demo
# Example output: Waiting for deployment 'probe-demo' rollout to finish: 1 out of 3 new replicas have been updated...

# 3. Check for failed Pods (ErrImagePull or ImagePullBackOff)
kubectl get pod -l app=probe-demo
# → nginx:broken-image Pods show ErrImagePull/ImagePullBackOff
#   The existing nginx:1.27 Pods (3) are still Running (thanks to maxUnavailable:0)

# 4. View revision history
kubectl rollout history deployment/probe-demo
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         v1 initial deployment - nginx 1.25
# 2         v2 deployment - nginx 1.27 / maxUnavailable=0 stable rollout
# 3         v3 deployment - bad image (intentional failure for lab)

# 5. Roll back to revision 2 (nginx:1.27)
kubectl rollout undo deployment/probe-demo --to-revision=2
# Output: deployment.apps/probe-demo rolled back

# 6. Confirm rollback is complete
kubectl rollout status deployment/probe-demo
kubectl get pod -l app=probe-demo
# → All 3 Pods running nginx:1.27

# Verify the current image
kubectl get deployment probe-demo -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'
# Output: nginx:1.27

# Final clean-up
kubectl delete deployment probe-demo
tasks.explanation: The maxUnavailable: 0 setting proves its value in this failure scenario. The bad image (nginx:broken-image) stays in ImagePullBackOff and never becomes Ready, so its Readiness Probe never passes. Because maxUnavailable: 0 requires a new Pod to be Ready before any old Pod can be terminated, Kubernetes refuses to touch the existing nginx:1.27 Pods. The rollout stalls, but the service keeps running. rollout undo --to-revision=2 restores the saved revision 2 ReplicaSet (nginx:1.27) to DESIRED=3 and scales the broken-image RS back to 0. This rollback follows the same rolling mechanism (respecting maxSurge/maxUnavailable), so it is also zero-downtime. Note that after the rollback, the revision number does not revert to 2 — it advances to a new number (4), which you can verify with rollout history.
hints: Pressing Ctrl+C while kubectl rollout status is waiting does not cancel the deployment itself — it only stops the watch. To actually revert the deployment, you must run rollout undo.

Always check kubectl rollout history for the actual REVISION numbers before specifying --to-revision. If you followed these labs in order, nginx:1.27 should be revision 2, but the number may differ if you redeployed at any point.

After the rollback, run kubectl get deployment probe-demo -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}' to confirm the image is nginx:1.27. Make it a habit to verify the active image after every rollback.
